serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve snack portion of raisins is approximately 43 g (about 1/4 cup or one small snack box).

nutrient_source.title: Raisins, seedless — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168165)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for seedless raisins used as the primary nutrient reference. Input per_100g values from HPB were critically reviewed and found to be implausible (e.g., energy 15 kcal, calcium 746 mg, iron 101 mg, cholesterol 301 mg for raisins are clearly erroneous). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy values are used as the authoritative replacement. Singapore HPB database does not carry a raisin entry with reliable macro data. USDA values are consistent with established food composition literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; raisins entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 64 for raisins (glucose reference = 100) is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Foster-Powell et al., Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 64 × (79.18 × 0.43 - 3.7 × 0.43) / 100 = 64 × 32.56 / 100 ≈ 20.8; rounded to 20.1 using net carbohydrate of 75.48 g/100g × 43 g serving = 32.46 g net carbs; GL = 64 × 32.46 / 100 = 20.8 ≈ 20.1 per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (59.2 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; potassium content (749 mg/100g) is beneficial but does not offset sugar-driven blood pressure risk at typical serving sizes
hypertension.guidance: Limit raisins to no more than 15–20 g per occasion; their high sugar load can raise insulin levels and promote sodium retention, partially negating the blood-pressure benefit of their potassium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 59.2 g/100g and GI 64 with a glycemic load of ~20 per 43 g serving; concentrated dried fruit causes rapid and significant postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raisins should be strictly limited or avoided by people with type 2 diabetes — a 43 g serving delivers approximately 20 glycemic load units, causing significant blood glucose spikes; if used, restrict to ≤15 g and pair with protein or fat to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.06 g/100g) and zero cholesterol are favourable; however, very high sugar (59.2 g/100g) raises triglycerides and contributes to metabolic cardiovascular risk at typical snack portions
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raisins are low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, which is cardioprotective, but their very high sugar content can elevate triglycerides and promote metabolic syndrome; limit to small portions (≤20 g) and avoid daily consumption as a standalone snack.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar 59.2 g/100g far exceeds the 15 g/100g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Avoid raisins as a regular snack for rheumatoid arthritis; if used, restrict to ≤10–15 g as a flavour accent only, and prefer fresh grapes or berries as lower-sugar anti-inflammatory alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raisins are dried grapes produced by dehydrating fresh grapes — most commonly Thompson Seedless (Vitis vinifera) — through sun-drying or mechanical drying. The drying process concentrates all nutrients and sugars, resulting in a small, wrinkled, dark-brown to purple fruit with an intensely sweet, slightly tangy flavour and a soft, chewy texture. Per 100 g, raisins provide approximately 299 kcal, 79 g carbohydrate (of which 59 g is sugar), 3.7 g dietary fibre, 3.1 g protein, and only 0.46 g fat. They are an excellent source of potassium (749 mg/100g) and provide useful amounts of iron, B vitamins, and phosphorus. The glycemic index is 64 (medium), but the glycemic load per standard 43 g serving is approximately 20, making them a high-glycaemic-load snack in practice. Raisins are widely used globally in baking (bread, cakes, cookies, muffins), cooking (rice pilafs, tagines, chutneys), breakfast cereals, trail mixes, and as standalone snacks. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, raisins are unsuitable for people with type 2 diabetes or those managing blood glucose due to their very high sugar concentration and significant glycaemic load per serving. Their high sugar content also raises concern for hypertension (insulin-mediated sodium retention) and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory sugar load), despite beneficial potassium and low saturated fat content.
common_user_queries: Are raisins good for blood pressure?
How much sugar is in raisins?
What is the glycemic index of raisins?
Can I eat raisins on a low-carb diet?
Are raisins the same as sultanas?
How many raisins in a serving?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168165), an approved primary source with exact match for seedless raisins. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed International GI Tables (Foster-Powell et al., 2008). Input HPB per_100g values were critically identified as erroneous (energy 15 kcal, calcium 746 mg, iron 101 mg, cholesterol 301 mg are physiologically impossible for raisins) and replaced with USDA-verified values. High confidence is warranted given exact USDA source match.
notes: INPUT DATA CORRECTION: The input per_100g values from HPB were critically erroneous and have been replaced entirely with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy values (FDC ID 168165). Specific errors in input data: energy 15.0 kcal (correct: ~299 kcal), protein 0.0 g (correct: 3.07 g), fat 3.28 g (correct: 0.46 g), carbohydrate 0.06 g (correct: 79.18 g), calcium 746.0 mg (correct: 50 mg), iron 101.0 mg (correct: 1.88 mg), cholesterol 301.0 mg (correct: 0 mg). These input values appear to be data entry errors or misassigned records. ZERO-VALUE JUSTIFICATION: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; raisins are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as raisins are a plant food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA for dried grapes. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; raisins contain no industrially produced trans fats. DIETARY TAGS CORRECTION: Input dietary_tags included 'low_calorie' and 'keto' which are incorrect for raisins (299 kcal/100g, 79 g carbohydrate/100g); these tags have been removed. MULTILINGUAL NOTE: Raisin is a globally used English-origin food term; no mandatory local-language names apply as this is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish. G6 multilingual gate is satisfied as no mandatory language rules apply to this food category. ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code cited are defensible standard references for raisins; FoodEx2 code A00LN is the standard EFSA FoodEx2 code for raisins/dried grapes.
